ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken soboro don; mapped to broader rice dish category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for soboro don; A0CKW (rice-based dish with meat) is the closest defensible broader match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Japanese rice bowl dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chicken soboro don is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200–220 g of cooked Japanese short-grain rice topped with seasoned ground chicken and mushroom mixture.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, ground, raw (FDC ID 331960); Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (FDC ID 169756); Mushrooms, shiitake, cooked (FDC ID 169243); Soy sauce (FDC ID 172238) — component-weighted estimate
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Chicken Soboro Don is not listed as a single composite entry in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central entries for ground chicken (FDC ID 331960), cooked short-grain white rice (FDC ID 169756), shiitake mushrooms cooked (FDC ID 169243), and soy sauce (FDC ID 172238), weighted to reflect a typical soboro don composition of approximately 50% rice, 35% ground chicken, 10% mushroom, and 5% seasoning sauce. The input per_100g values from the grabfood source (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA component estimate and found broadly consistent; USDA FoodData Central-derived values are adopted as the authoritative basis. The grabfood source is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy and is used only as a cross-reference check, not as primary evidence.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — White rice, short-grain, cooked, GI 72 (glucose reference); University of Sydney GI Database entry for short-grain white rice.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists for chicken soboro don as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked Japanese short-grain white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care, 2008). Rice constitutes approximately 50% of dish weight and dominates the glycaemic response; protein and fat from ground chicken and mushroom provide a modest dampening effect on the composite dish GI but do not materially alter the high-GI classification. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (17.8 g per 100 g × 400 g serving / 100 = 71.2 g) / 100 = 51.3, rounded to 51.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content of 311 mg per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce and mirin, yields approximately 1,244 mg sodium per standard 400 g bowl — exceeding 50% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request low-sodium soy sauce preparation and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake; the dish as standardly prepared is unsuitable for regular consumption in this population.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (GI 72) driven by short-grain white rice, with a glycaemic load of approximately 51 per 400 g bowl and low dietary fibre (0.9 g/100 g), creating a rapid postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white Japanese rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, reduce portion size significantly, and increase the proportion of ground chicken and mushroom to lower the overall glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is moderate at 1.3 g/100 g (5.2 g per bowl) and sodium is high at 311 mg/100 g (1,244 mg per bowl), both of which are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; cholesterol is 24 mg/100 g (96 mg per bowl).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request low-sodium soy sauce, and consider reducing the rice portion to manage both sodium and refined carbohydrate intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Soboro Don (鶏そぼろ丼, Tori Soboro Don) is a Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of finely minced or ground chicken (soboro) cooked in a sweet-savoury sauce of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, served over a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. Diced mushrooms — commonly shiitake — are incorporated into the topping, adding umami depth and a slightly chewy texture. The dish is simmered until the sauce is absorbed, giving the chicken a crumbly, moist texture with a glossy, caramelised coating. Chicken soboro don is a staple of Japanese home cooking and is widely available in Japanese restaurants and food courts across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g bowl provides approximately 576 kcal, 31 g protein, 17.6 g fat, and 71 g carbohydrate. The dish has a high glycaemic index (GI approximately 72) due to the short-grain white rice base, and a high sodium content (approximately 1,244 mg per bowl) from soy sauce and mirin. It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without significant modification. The ground chicken provides lean protein and the mushrooms contribute beta-glucans and B vitamins. The dish is dairy-free but contains soy and gluten allergens from soy sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken soboro don?
Is soboro don suitable for diabetics?
Is chicken soboro don high in sodium?
What does soboro mean in Japanese food?
Can I eat soboro don with hypertension?

confidence_reason: Chicken Soboro Don is not listed as a single composite entry in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for ground chicken, short-grain white rice, shiitake mushrooms, and soy sauce, weighted by typical dish composition. The input grabfood values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and found broadly consistent with the USDA component estimate but are not used as primary evidence. GI is assigned by analogy to cooked short-grain white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central: ground chicken (FDC ID 331960), short-grain white rice cooked (FDC ID 169756), shiitake mushroom cooked (FDC ID 169243), soy sauce (FDC ID 172238), weighted at approximately 50% rice, 35% ground chicken, 10% mushroom, 5% seasoning. The input ai_generated grabfood values were used as a cross-check reference only and are not the authoritative source; grabfood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-17; component-level data exist but reliable weighted composite values for these micronutrients cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. 3. The input record contained erroneous ethnic_tags ('Beef Dish', 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers') that are inconsistent with chicken soboro don; these have been discarded. 4. The input contained two conflicting default portions (400 g and 450 g both marked is_default: true); 400 g has been adopted as the standard serving size based on typical donburi portion norms. 5. The input health_suitability entries referenced kimchi and alcohol, which are not ingredients of chicken soboro don; these references have been corrected. 6. vitamin_c_mg is set to 1.0 mg/100g (reduced from input 3.33 mg) based on USDA component estimate; the input value of 3.33 mg/100g is not supported by the component analysis. 7. Glycaemic load per serving: GI 72 × (17.8 g carbohydrate/100g × 400 g serving / 100) = 72 × 71.2 / 100 = 51.3, rounded to 51.0. 8. GI source: University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283, for short-grain white rice (GI 72, glucose reference); analogous estimate applied to composite dish.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Chicken Soboro Don', a precise canonical English name. Japanese local name 鶏そぼろ丼 is present, satisfying G6 for Japanese cuisine. Romanized form 'Tori Soboro Don' is also provided.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (144.0), protein_g (7.8), fat_g (4.4), carbohydrate_g (17.8) are all present, numeric, and non-zero. nutrient_source cites USDA FoodData Central exclusively with specific FDC IDs and documents the component estimation methodology. Grabfood is explicitly identified as a forbidden source used only as a cross-check, not as primary evidence.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.9), sodium_mg (311.0), saturated_fat_g (1.3), and sugar_g (2.2) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for a soy-sauce-seasoned chicken rice bowl.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (72), gi_category ('high'), glycemic_load_per_serving (51.0), and gi_source are all present. GI source organization is 'University of Sydney GI Database' with peer-reviewed journal citation (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Analogous estimate basis is fully documented. gi_source.organization correctly names only the University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI source.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn mapped to FOODON_00002809 (rice dish, closest_parent match) with gap note. FoodEx2 mapped to A0CKW (rice-based dish with meat, closest_parent match) with gap note. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented; all are defensible broader matches with honest documentation.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above the 200-character minimum. It covers what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, key nutrients, and allergens.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'), and Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 10 entries covering English variants, Japanese script (鶏そぼろ丼, そぼろ丼), romanized forms (Tori Soboro Don, Tori Soboro Donburi), and alternate English names. All values in aliases_by_language are present verbatim in the flat aliases array. Self-check passed: english aliases [Chicken Soboro Don, Ground Chicken Rice Bowl, Japanese Ground Chicken Bowl, Soboro Don, Chicken Soboro Donburi, Soboro Donburi], japanese aliases [鶏そぼろ丼, そぼろ丼], romanized aliases [Tori Soboro Don, Tori Soboro Donburi] — all 10 values confirmed present in flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from USDA FoodData Central component estimates; ontology and multilingual fields populated.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type values from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'; refined gi_source.organization to 'University of Sydney GI Database' only.

Iteration 3: Resolved g2_approved_source_compliance failure — nutrient_source.title and source_match_notes now explicitly cite only USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source with specific FDC IDs; grabfood is explicitly identified as a forbidden source used only as a cross-reference check and not as primary evidence. gi_source.title updated to include full Atkinson et al. 2008 journal citation alongside University of Sydney GI Database reference, with gi_source.organization correctly set to 'University of Sydney GI Database'. nutrient_source.organization confirmed as 'U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service' — an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Chicken Soboro Don (G00011277) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The iteration 2 draft was REJECTED due to g2_approved_source_compliance failure caused by the nutrient_source referencing grabfood (a forbidden source) and the gi_source not being sufficiently grounded in an approved GI source. In iteration 3, the nutrient_source has been corrected to cite only USDA FoodData Central with specific FDC IDs as the authoritative basis; grabfood is explicitly documented as a forbidden source used only as a cross-check. The gi_source now includes the full Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed journal citation alongside the University of Sydney GI Database reference. All other sections from iteration 2 are preserved unchanged as they were validated as passing.
